title: Notes from The Power of Statistics — Course 3
summary: Key takeaways from completing Course 3 of the Google Advanced Data Analytics certificate — probability distributions, confidence intervals, and why hypothesis testing matters for real data work.
body:
Just finished Course 3 of the Google Advanced Data Analytics certificate — The Power of Statistics. This one felt like the biggest conceptual jump so far, because unlike cleaning data or building visualizations, statistics requires you to think carefully about what you can and cannot claim from a dataset.

The ideas that took the most work to internalize:

Probability distributions describe how likely different outcomes are. The normal distribution comes up constantly in data analytics because many real-world measurements (heights, errors, averages of large samples) naturally follow it. Once you understand that, a lot of statistical formulas stop feeling arbitrary and start making sense.

Confidence intervals are not as intuitive as they sound. A 95% confidence interval does not mean there is a 95% chance the true value is inside it — it means that if you repeated this sampling process many times, 95% of the intervals you built would contain the true value. That distinction matters when you are communicating findings to someone who might misread certainty into your numbers.

Hypothesis testing gave me a framework for answering a question I had not previously known how to ask properly: is this difference I am seeing in the data real, or is it plausibly just random variation? The p-value answers that question, but only if you set your significance threshold before looking at the data — not after.

The practical takeaway: statistics does not tell you what is true. It tells you how much evidence you have for a claim, and how likely you are to be wrong. That is a more useful thing to know.
